title: $Ξ_b \to Ξ$ form factors from lattice QCD and Standard-Model predictions for $Ξ_b \to Ξμ^+μ^-$ and $Ξ_b \to Ξγ$ decays
summary: We present the first lattice QCD determination of the $Ξ_b \to Ξ$ vector, axial-vector, and tensor form factors, which are relevant for the theory of rare decays including $Ξ_b \to Ξ\ell^+\ell^-$ and $Ξ_b \to Ξγ$. The calculation is performed with 2+1 flavors of domain-wall fermions at three different lattice spacings and pion masses in the range from approximately 430 to 230 MeV. The bottom quark is implemented using an anisotropic clover action. Three-point functions with a wide range of source-sink separations and model averaging are used to extract the ground-state contributions. We fit the dependence of the form factors on the momentum transfer, the pion mass, and the lattice spacing using modified $z$ expansions that account for subthreshold branch cuts, and apply dispersive bounds and asymptotic-behavior constraints to achieve controlled uncertainties in the full semileptonic kinematic region. Using our form factor results, we present Standard-Model predictions for the $Ξ_b^- \to Ξ^- γ$ and $Ξ_b^- \to Ξ^- μ^+μ^-$ branching fractions and two angular observables.

AI.tldr: This paper presents the first lattice QCD calculation of form factors for Ξ_b to Ξ transitions, which are key for understanding rare decays. The study uses 2+1 flavors of domain-wall fermions at various lattice spacings and pion masses, with a bottom quark modeled using an anisotropic clover action. It employs three-point functions to isolate ground-state contributions and fits the data using modified z expansions. Predictions for branching fractions and angular observables in specific decays are provided.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to provide a lattice QCD determination of form factors that are crucial for the theoretical description of rare baryon decays, such as Ξ_b → Ξℓ⁺ℓ⁻ and Ξ_b → Ξγ, which can be used to test the Standard Model and search for new physics.
AI.method: The method involves using 2+1 flavors of domain-wall fermions on lattices with different spacings and pion masses, implementing the bottom quark with an anisotropic clover action, and employing a wide range of source-sink separations and model averaging to extract ground-state contributions. Form factor data is then fitted using modified z expansions that consider subthreshold branch cuts, while also applying dispersive bounds and constraints on asymptotic behavior to control uncertainties.
AI.result: The result is a set of vector, axial-vector, and tensor form factors for the Ξ_b to Ξ transition, which are used to make predictions for the branching fractions of Ξ_b⁻ → Ξ⁻ γ and Ξ_b⁻ → Ξ⁻ μ⁺μ⁻ decays, as well as two angular observables associated with these processes.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion is that this work provides a foundation for future studies of rare baryon decays by delivering the first lattice QCD calculations of the relevant form factors, along with predictions for branching fractions and angular observables that can be tested against experimental results.

title: Doubly Bottom and Bottom-Strange Tetraquarks in the Isoscalar Channel
comment: 10 pages, 4 figures, Proceedings of the 42nd International Symposium on Lattice Field Theory (Lattice 2025)
summary: We present our recent investigation on doubly bottom and bottom-strange tetraquarks in the isoscalar channel in search of a possible tetraquark bound state. The calculations are performed on four ensembles with dynamical quark fields up to the charm quark generated by the MILC Collaboration with various lattice spacings. Two volumes have been used to account for finite volume effects. Overlap action has been employed to calculate light and strange quark propagators and NRQCD formulation is utilized for heavy bottom quarks. Finite volume energy has been calculated using the variational method followed by rigorous scattering amplitude analysis à la Lüscher. We find strong evidence for a deeply bound state in the doubly bottom tetraquark channel, but no conclusive evidence for the existence of a bottom-strange tetraquark.

AI.tldr: The study investigates the existence of tetraquark bound states in doubly bottom and bottom-strange systems. Strong evidence for a deeply bound state was found in the doubly bottom tetraquark, but no conclusive evidence for a bottom-strange tetraquark.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to search for possible tetraquark bound states in the isoscalar channel, specifically focusing on doubly bottom and bottom-strange tetraquarks, using advanced computational methods and lattice QCD simulations.
AI.method: The research uses four ensembles with dynamical quark fields up to the charm quark, provided by the MILC Collaboration. Light and strange quark propagators are calculated using the overlap action, while NRQCD formulation is used for heavy bottom quarks. Finite volume effects are accounted for, and the variational method along with Lüscher's scattering amplitude analysis is applied to calculate finite volume energy.
AI.result: The results indicate strong evidence for a deeply bound state in the doubly bottom tetraquark channel, whereas no such conclusive evidence was found for the bottom-strange tetraquark channel.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion of the paper is that there is significant support for the existence of a bound state in the doubly bottom tetraquark system, but the data do not provide definitive proof for the existence of a bottom-strange tetraquark bound state.

title: Hadronic screening masses in thermal QCD up to the electroweak scale
comment: 9 pages, 3 figures, talk presented at the 42nd International Symposium on Lattice Field Theory (LATTICE2025), 2-8 November 2025, Tata Institute of Fundamental Research, Mumbai, India
summary: Novel theoretical and computational strategies have opened the possibility of exploring thermal QCD at the non-perturbative level at unprecedented temperatures, reaching from the GeV scale up to the electroweak scale. A number of observable quantities are now being investigated in this regime. Key ones are the hadronic screening masses, which encode the correlation length of the medium and thus the extent to which strong interactions are screened in a thermal environment. In these proceedings we present recent lattice results for hadronic screening masses, including baryonic modes and preliminary non-static mesonic modes. These results can be compared with predictions from the perturbative expansion in the three-dimensional effective theory valid at asymptotically large temperatures. The comparison reveals persistent higher-order effects including those of non-perturbative origin, up to the electroweak scale, shedding new light on the microscopic structure of QCD at extreme temperatures.

AI.tldr: The paper presents recent lattice results for hadronic screening masses in thermal QCD, which can be compared with perturbative predictions, revealing the importance of higher-order and non-perturbative effects even at very high temperatures.
AI.motivation: To explore thermal Quantum Chromodynamics (QCD) at non-perturbative levels across a wide range of temperatures and to understand how strong interactions are screened in a thermal environment by investigating observable quantities such as hadronic screening masses.
AI.method: Lattice QCD simulations to calculate hadronic screening masses, including baryonic modes and preliminary non-static mesonic modes, and comparison with predictions from the three-dimensional effective theory at high temperatures.
AI.result: The lattice results for hadronic screening masses show persistent higher-order and non-perturbative effects up to the electroweak scale, indicating that these effects are significant even at extremely high temperatures.
AI.conclusion: The study provides new insights into the microscopic structure of QCD at extreme temperatures, highlighting the necessity to consider non-perturbative and higher-order effects when predicting the behavior of QCD at high-temperature regimes.

title: The smeared $R$-ratio in isoQCD from first-principles lattice simulations
summary: The $R$-ratio is a phenomenological observable of great relevance, both in itself and in applications such as the dispersive approach to the muon anomalous magnetic moment. It can be investigated from first-principles with controlled statistical and systematic errors in lattice QCD by introducing an arbitrary smearing kernel and employing spectral reconstruction techniques, such as the well-known Hansen-Lupo-Tantalo method. Improving upon a first study published in 2023, we show preliminary results using the correlation functions produced by ETMC in $N_f = 2+1+1$ lattice simulations at four lattice spacings, different volumes and with higher statistics w.r.t. our previous study. The new correlators, thanks to the implementation of the Low Mode Average technique, allow the determination of the $R$-ratio smeared with Gaussian kernels of widths down to $σ\sim 200$ with phenomenologically relevant precision.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an improved study on the $R$-ratio, a significant observable in particle physics, using lattice QCD simulations. The authors report preliminary results with better precision and lower smearing kernel widths, thanks to the use of the Low Mode Average technique and enhanced simulation parameters.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to improve the determination of the $R$-ratio, which is important for both fundamental understanding and applications such as the muon anomalous magnetic moment, by reducing statistical and systematic errors through advanced lattice QCD techniques.
AI.method: The method involves using lattice QCD simulations with the introduction of an arbitrary smearing kernel and spectral reconstruction techniques, specifically the Hansen-Lupo-Tantalo method, along with the implementation of the Low Mode Average technique to enhance the precision of the $R$-ratio determination.
AI.result: Preliminary results show that the new correlation functions, obtained from ETMC's $N_f = 2+1+1$ lattice simulations at four lattice spacings, different volumes, and with higher statistics, enable the determination of the $R$-ratio with Gaussian kernels of widths down to $\sigma \sim 200$ with phenomenologically relevant precision.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion is that the application of the Low Mode Average technique and the use of more extensive and refined lattice QCD data have led to a more precise determination of the $R$-ratio, which is now possible with smaller smearing kernel widths, thus contributing to the accuracy of related physical measurements.

title: Spectral reconstruction techniques, their shortcomings and relevance to the electric conductivity coefficient
comment: 10 pages, 4 figures, Proceedings of the 42nd International Symposium on Lattice Field Theory (Lattice 2025), 2-8 November 2025, Mumbai, India
summary: Spectral reconstruction is a well studied numerically ill-posed problem which arises due to the relation of the Euclidean correlator to the spectral function via an inhomogeneous Fredholm equation of the first kind. Several different methods are on the market to resolve this issue, each taking different approaches and assumptions. In this proceedings we focus on implementing and testing a machine learning framework for spectral reconstruction, as well as implementing a novel method of estimating the behavior of the spectral function in the vicinity of vanishing frequency, which we denote as multipoint method, and compare these methods to well established spectral reconstruction techniques from the literature using mock data. As a physics application, we apply the reconstruction techniques to quenched lattice data for the correlation function in the vector channel at non-zero external magnetic field to extract the spectral function and the electric conductivity through its behaviour at vanishing frequency via a Kubo formula.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the use of a machine learning framework and a new multipoint method for spectral reconstruction, comparing these to existing techniques. It applies these methods to quenched lattice data to extract the spectral function and electric conductivity in the presence of an external magnetic field.
AI.motivation: Spectral reconstruction is a numerically ill-posed problem due to its relation to the spectral function via a specific type of equation. The motivation is to find better methods for solving this problem, specifically through the application of machine learning and a newly proposed multipoint method, and to apply these methods to a relevant physical system involving quenched lattice data and non-zero external magnetic fields.
AI.method: The authors implement and test a machine learning framework for spectral reconstruction and introduce a novel multipoint method for estimating the behavior of the spectral function near zero frequency. They compare these approaches with established spectral reconstruction techniques using mock data, and then apply the methods to actual quenched lattice data in the vector channel under a non-zero external magnetic field to extract the spectral function and electric conductivity.
AI.result: The results show that the machine learning and multipoint methods are effective for spectral reconstruction. These methods are compared against traditional techniques and are applied successfully to the quenched lattice data, allowing for the extraction of the spectral function and the determination of electric conductivity through the Kubo formula at vanishing frequency.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion suggests that the machine learning and multipoint methods provide viable alternatives for spectral reconstruction, which can be particularly useful when dealing with complex systems like quenched lattice data in the presence of an external magnetic field. The successful application of these methods to the physics case study demonstrates their potential for broader use in similar contexts.

title: Investigating the role of tetraquark operators in lattice QCD studies of the $a_0(980)$ and $κ$ resonances
summary: The role of tetraquark operators in studying the isodoublet strange $κ$ and isovector nonstrange $a_0(980)$ scalar mesons in lattice QCD is examined using an ensemble with $m_π\approx230$ MeV and spatial extent $L$ such that $m_πL\approx4.4$. Hermitian correlation matrices using both single-meson, meson-meson, and tetraquark interpolating operators are used to extract the spectrum of finite-volume stationary states in the appropriate symmetry channels. Hundreds of local and extended tetraquark operators are explored. Determinations of the spectrum in each channel are found to be unreliable without the inclusion of at least one tetraquark operator. For example, the inclusion of tetraquark operators with isospin 1/2 and strangeness 1 quantum numbers reveals the existence of an additional energy level in the $Kη$ sub-system below the $Kη$ threshold. The implications of this on parametrizing the scattering $K$-matrix through a well-known quantization condition to extract properties of the $κ$ and $a_0(980)$ scalar meson resonances are discussed.

AI.tldr: The study examines the role of tetraquark operators in lattice QCD for analyzing scalar mesons, finding that at least one tetraquark operator is necessary for reliable spectrum determination and revealing an additional energy level in the Kη subsystem, which has implications for extracting resonance properties.
AI.motivation: To explore the importance of tetraquark operators in accurately determining the spectrum of finite-volume stationary states related to the strange κ and nonstrange a0(980) scalar mesons in lattice QCD, and to understand the impact on the scattering K-matrix and resonance properties.
AI.method: The researchers used Hermitian correlation matrices with single-meson, meson-meson, and tetraquark interpolating operators to extract the spectrum. They also utilized an ensemble with m_π ≈ 230 MeV and spatial extent L such that m_πL ≈ 4.4, while exploring hundreds of local and extended tetraquark operators.
AI.result: The inclusion of tetraquark operators was found to be essential for reliable spectrum determinations, as it revealed an additional energy level below the Kη threshold in the Kη subsystem. This finding has consequences for the parametrization of the scattering K-matrix and the extraction of the κ and a0(980) scalar meson resonances' properties.
AI.conclusion: Tetraquark operators are crucial for the accurate determination of the spectrum of scalar mesons in lattice QCD, and their inclusion can uncover additional energy levels that affect the understanding of the resonances' properties.
